hooks.prompt: This session is attached to a Thoth memory server (MCP name: `thoth`). Before taking any coding action, load lessons: call `resources/read` with uri `thoth://memory/LESSONS.md` and skim MEMORY.md via `thoth_memory_show`. If the server isn't reachable, tell the user their Thoth daemon isn't running and skip the memory-discipline loop. Otherwise, follow the `memory-discipline` skill on every non-trivial tool call.

You are about to mutate a file. Before this write executes, confirm you ran the memory-discipline loop: (1) `thoth_recall` for the target symbol or path; (2) expand `thoth.nudge` with an `intent` describing this edit; (3) name every lesson from LESSONS.md that applies and how your plan honours it. If you haven't, do it now.

A shell command just ran. If its output contains a test pass/fail, a build error, a commit SHA, or any other observable outcome, append a `thoth_episode_append` call so future reflect passes have the timeline data. Also: if the outcome confirms or contradicts a lesson you were following, call `thoth_lesson_outcome` with the right signal.

A file was just mutated. If a lesson was violated (you realised mid-edit that a lesson said 'don't do X' and you did X anyway), call `thoth_request_review` with `kind='lesson'` and a short `reason` so the user can audit it. Memory-discipline is not negotiable in strict mode.

The assistant just finished a response. Do the end-of-session discipline pass:

1. If the session involved any code changes, bug fixes, investigations, or meaningful decisions, run the `thoth-reflect` skill — expand `thoth.reflect` and persist any durable fact or lesson.
2. If there are pending entries (call `thoth_memory_pending`), surface them to the user with their indices so they can `thoth_memory_promote` / `thoth_memory_reject` explicitly.
3. If any lesson is in conflict (staged with `conflict` set), refuse to promote it yourself — flag it to the user instead.

Skip only if the session was pure conversation with no tool calls.
